summary:
There is no scientific literature reporting glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) as a plasminogen receptor in pathogenic bacteria. Pathogens instead use specialized surface proteins such as HvgA for plasminogen binding, which facilitates tissue invasion through proteolytic activity. 

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{No}}$

The summary focuses only on clinically significant findings related to the query about GAPDH serving as a plasmanogen receptor in pathogens — specifically noting absence of reports and highlighting established alternatives involved in plasminogen interactions among infectious agents. This directly addresses whether GAPDH has an identified functional role beyond metabolism within microbial pathogenesis pathways concerning extracellular matrix degradation via plasmin activation.
